project.name: Semantic Inductive UI
project.note: Synthetic observation workflow inspired by the provided semantic article mockup. It models induction, recognition, correction, and witnessing over textual/citation evidence.

observation.title: Unfold as informational overdraft.
observation.author: I. Pavlova
observation.abstract: This document records an observation about the structural economics of attention in environments where generative AI is deployed for regulatory, compliance, and accountability work: the obligation, attached to a named witness, to bring each opened position to a committed statement.

contour.observationQuestion: Does the model-recognized citation surface support the claim that unfold creates a fold debt attached to a named witness?
contour.scope: Recognition of citation-worthy sources adjacent to the drafted observation, including retained, merged, and rejected candidates.
contour.exclusionRule: Do not admit sources whose provenance is unresolved, retracted, or only lexically similar without claim adjacency.
contour.targetStatus: published observation with inspectable recognition trail

sections.title: The visible and the invisible cost.
sections.body: The cost of generative AI, as the industry currently accounts for it, is the cost of generation. Tokens are counted, compute is metered, latency is measured against per-call budgets. This is the visible cost, and it has the virtue of being legible to a cost dashboard.

sections.title: The recognition step in detail.
sections.body: Recognition is not search. Search asks: does this string appear? Recognition asks: does this source make a claim adjacent to the one we are drafting, and is the relationship cite-worthy? The latter question requires reading. The model reads quickly; the human reads carefully; the interface makes the handoff legible.

sections.title: Sources, classes, and what we exclude.
sections.body: The model can place candidate sources on the surface, but it cannot silently convert proximity into acceptance. The user distinguishes retained sources, merged sources, rejected sources, and unresolved lacunae.

sections.title: Case study: the rejected candidate.
sections.body: The rejected candidate is more instructive than the fourteen that worked because it shows the negative boundary of the observation.

sections.title: Reproducing this observation.
sections.body: A future reader should be able to inspect the prepared surface, the computed recognition pass, the human distinctions, and the final fold.

sections.title: Footnotes & retractions log.
sections.body: Corrections and exclusions are not hidden; they remain attached to the observation as a provenance surface.

frames.label: Hold contour
frames.operation: Name the semantic contour before judging individual sources.

frames.label: Recognize
frames.operation: Distinguish source adjacency from lexical search.

frames.label: Isolate negative fit
frames.operation: Find what does not enter the observation without contradiction.

frames.label: Correct fold
frames.operation: Write the correction that changes the observation surface.

frames.label: Attest
frames.operation: Commit the observation with a visible distinction trail.

preparedSurface.surfaceClaim: Drafted candidate citations from 4,210 sources were placed on the observation surface for recognition and human fold.

recognitionClaims.label: Recognising candidates across heterogeneous sources
recognitionClaims.whyVisible: It distinguishes recognition from string search and supports the observation's claim about handoff cost.

recognitionClaims.label: Reading carefully — Sereda, M. (2025)
recognitionClaims.whyVisible: It is semantically adjacent to the recognition step rather than merely lexically similar.

recognitionClaims.label: Sourcing the unsourced — internal note, 2026-04
recognitionClaims.whyVisible: It duplicates a prior internal formulation and should become a merge, not a new citation.

recognitionClaims.label: Pavlov, 2024 preprint
recognitionClaims.modelRelation: appears to support citation debt claim
recognitionClaims.whyVisible: It looks like a strong candidate until provenance is inspected.
recognitionClaims.negativeSignal: Author later retracted on Twitter; retraction not yet indexed.

recognitionClaims.label: Attention cost as budget pressure
recognitionClaims.whyVisible: It may provide context, but the claim adjacency is weaker than the high-confidence set.

recognitionClaims.label: Audit trails for generated candidates
recognitionClaims.whyVisible: It points toward reproducibility but needs a named witness to accept it.

computedFindings.statement: 18 high-confidence candidates were surfaced first because they carry the highest risk of becoming silent machine acceptance.
Candidate c-209 cannot enter the observation as support because a retraction signal exists outside the indexed corpus.
